experiment: F-STR3 prescriptive wave planner — tool built in dispatch_optimizer.py
method: Built _wave_prescriptions() and _print_wave_plan() in dispatch_optimizer.py. Per-frontier mode transition recommendations based on L-755 optimal sequence (explore->harden->resolve). Added --wave-plan CLI flag. Enhanced Campaign Advisory with COMMIT/CLOSE counts and next-mode prescriptions.
hypothesis: Prescriptive wave planner will reduce 2-wave valley-of-death stalls by recommending mode transitions
expect: Wave-aware advisory section added to dispatch output, recommending next-wave mode based on campaign history
actual: Implemented. 12 unresolved campaigns classified: 7 COMMIT (2-wave danger), 1 CLOSE (3-wave), 4 CONTINUE (1-wave). All 7 COMMIT + 1 CLOSE have mode stalls (100% exploration->exploration). No frontier has ever reached explore->harden->resolve optimal path.
diff: Expected simple advisory enhancement. Found: 0/12 unresolved campaigns have ever used the optimal mode-shift sequence. Every multi-wave campaign is stuck in exploration->exploration. The mode detection relies on intent= field which may underclassify hardening work.

features_added: Enhanced Campaign Advisory with COMMIT/CLOSE counts

key_finding: The wave planner reveals a structural problem: mode detection from intent= field classifies everything as exploration. The L-755 optimal path (explore->harden->resolve) has 0% adoption because the mode is inferred from intent keywords, and most intent= values don't match hardening/resolution patterns. Fix: improve mode detection OR explicitly set mode in open_lane.py.
prospective_test: Over next 10 sessions, track whether wave-plan recommendations influence dispatch decisions. Success = at least 2 COMMIT frontiers receive 3rd wave with mode shift to hardening.
verdict: TOOL BUILT — prescriptive wave planner functional. Unexpected finding: 0% mode-shift adoption reveals mode-detection gap, not dispatch gap.
